categories.name: Martyrs & Witnesses
categories.description: Those who gave their lives for the faith — from the early church through the Reformation.

people.name: Vibia Perpetua
people.title: Early Church Martyr
people.bio: Young noblewoman of Carthage martyred in the arena at age 22. Her diary, The Passion of Perpetua and Felicity, is one of the earliest Christian writings by a woman.
people.faith: When her father begged her to recant, she pointed to a water vessel and asked, 'Can you call a vase by any other name? Then I cannot be called by any other name than a Christian.' She went to her death with extraordinary courage.

sources.title: Perpetua — Wikipedia
sources.note: Account of the early church martyrs of Carthage

people.tagline: I cannot be called by any other name than a Christian

people.name: Felicity of Carthage
people.title: Early Church Martyr
people.bio: A slave and companion of Perpetua, martyred together in the Carthage arena. She gave birth in prison just days before her execution and refused to deny Christ.
people.faith: When mocked for crying out in labor pain, Felicity replied that in the arena, Christ would suffer in her. Her courage alongside Perpetua has inspired Christians for nearly two millennia.

sources.title: Felicity — Wikipedia
sources.note: Companion of Perpetua, martyred in Carthage

people.tagline: In the arena, another will be in me who will suffer for me

people.name: Polycarp of Smyrna
people.title: Bishop of Smyrna & Martyr
people.bio: Bishop of Smyrna and a direct disciple of the Apostle John. One of the earliest post-apostolic church fathers, he was burned at the stake at age 86 for refusing to worship the Roman emperor.
people.faith: When commanded to curse Christ, Polycarp declared: '86 years I have served Him, and He has done me no wrong. How can I blaspheme my King who saved me?' His martyrdom account is one of the earliest outside Scripture.

sources.title: Polycarp — Wikipedia
sources.note: Disciple of Apostle John, Bishop of Smyrna

people.tagline: 86 years I have served Him, and He has done me no wrong

people.name: Ignatius of Antioch
people.title: Bishop of Antioch & Martyr
people.bio: Third bishop of Antioch and early church father. Condemned to die in Rome, he was fed to wild beasts in the Colosseum. Wrote seven profound letters to churches while en route to his execution.
people.faith: Ignatius eagerly embraced martyrdom, writing: 'I am God's wheat, ground fine by the lion's teeth to be made purest bread for Christ.' His letters emphasize church unity, the Eucharist, and the reality of Christ's incarnation.

sources.title: Ignatius — Wikipedia
sources.note: Early church father and martyr

people.tagline: I am God's wheat, ground fine by the lion's teeth

people.name: Jan Hus
people.title: Bohemian Reformer & Martyr
people.bio: Czech priest and reformer who challenged church corruption a century before Luther. Burned at the stake at the Council of Constance after refusing to recant his teachings.
people.faith: Hus preached Scripture's authority over papal decrees and championed preaching in the common tongue. His followers, the Hussites, became forerunners of the Protestant Reformation. He reportedly said at his burning, 'You may roast this goose, but a swan will come whom you will not silence.'

sources.title: Jan Hus — Wikipedia
sources.note: Pre-Reformation Czech reformer

people.tagline: A forerunner of the Reformation, burned for the truth

people.name: William Tyndale
people.title: Bible Translator & Martyr
people.bio: English scholar who first translated the Bible into English from the original Hebrew and Greek. Strangled and burned at the stake in Belgium for heresy.
people.faith: Tyndale's dying prayer was 'Lord, open the King of England's eyes.' Within four years, Henry VIII authorized an English Bible based largely on Tyndale's translation. About 84% of his New Testament survives in the King James Version.

sources.title: Tyndale — Wikipedia
sources.note: Father of the English Bible

people.tagline: Lord, open the King of England's eyes

people.name: Hugh Latimer
people.title: Bishop & Reformation Martyr
people.bio: English Protestant bishop who became one of the leading preachers of the English Reformation. Burned at the stake in Oxford alongside Nicholas Ridley under Queen Mary I.
people.faith: As the flames were lit, Latimer spoke to Ridley: 'Be of good comfort, Master Ridley, and play the man! We shall this day light such a candle, by God's grace, in England, as I trust shall never be put out.' His words have echoed through centuries.

sources.title: Latimer — Wikipedia
sources.note: English Reformation martyr

people.tagline: We shall this day light such a candle as shall never be put out

people.name: Nicholas Ridley
people.title: Bishop of London & Reformation Martyr
people.bio: Bishop of London who helped shape the theological foundations of the Church of England. Burned at the stake alongside Hugh Latimer in Oxford.
people.faith: Ridley's scholarly work helped define Anglican theology, particularly regarding the Lord's Supper. He chose death over recanting his Protestant convictions, demonstrating that his faith was no mere intellectual exercise but a matter of eternal truth.

sources.title: Ridley — Wikipedia
sources.note: Bishop of London, burned with Latimer

people.tagline: Chose the flames over denying the truth

categories.description: Christians throughout history who glorified God through extraordinary works

people.name: Johann Sebastian Bach
people.title: Composer
people.bio: German composer and musician of the Baroque period, widely regarded as one of the greatest composers of all time. His works include the Brandenburg Concertos, the Mass in B minor, and hundreds of cantatas.
people.faith: A devout Lutheran who inscribed 'Soli Deo Gloria' (To God Alone Be the Glory) on his manuscripts. He composed extensively for the church and viewed his musical gifts as given by God for His glory.

people.name: George Frideric Handel
people.title: Composer
people.bio: German-born British Baroque composer famous for his operas, oratorios, and concertos. Best known for 'Messiah,' one of the most performed choral works in Western music.
people.faith: A committed Christian whose masterwork 'Messiah' sets to music the life of Jesus Christ from prophecy through resurrection. Upon completing the 'Hallelujah Chorus,' he reportedly said, 'I did think I did see all Heaven before me, and the great God Himself.'

people.name: Felix Mendelssohn
people.title: Composer
people.bio: German composer, pianist, and conductor of the early Romantic period. Known for his symphonies, concertos, and oratorios including 'Elijah.'
people.faith: Raised in a Jewish family that converted to Christianity, Mendelssohn was a devout Lutheran. His oratorio 'Elijah' and symphony 'Reformation' reflect his deep Christian faith. He also revived interest in Bach's sacred works.

people.name: Fanny Crosby
people.title: Hymn Writer
people.bio: American mission worker, poet, lyricist, and composer who wrote over 8,000 hymns and gospel songs. Blind from infancy, she became one of the most prolific hymnists in history.
people.faith: Despite losing her sight as an infant, Crosby devoted her life to glorifying God through music. She wrote beloved hymns including 'Blessed Assurance,' 'To God Be the Glory,' and 'All the Way My Savior Leads Me.'

people.name: C.S. Lewis
people.title: Author & Apologist
people.bio: British writer and lay theologian, author of 'The Chronicles of Narnia,' 'Mere Christianity,' and 'The Screwtape Letters.' Fellow of Magdalen College, Oxford and later Professor at Cambridge.
people.faith: A former atheist who converted to Christianity in 1931, Lewis became one of the most influential Christian apologists of the 20th century. He described his conversion in 'Surprised by Joy' and defended the faith in works like 'Mere Christianity.'

people.name: J.R.R. Tolkien
people.title: Author & Philologist
people.bio: English writer, philologist, and academic, best known for 'The Hobbit' and 'The Lord of the Rings.' Professor of Anglo-Saxon at Oxford University.
people.faith: A devout Roman Catholic whose faith deeply influenced his literary works. He was instrumental in C.S. Lewis's conversion to Christianity. He described 'The Lord of the Rings' as 'a fundamentally religious and Catholic work.'

people.name: John Milton
people.title: Poet
people.bio: English poet and intellectual who served as a civil servant during the Commonwealth. Best known for the epic poem 'Paradise Lost,' considered one of the greatest works in the English language.
people.faith: A deeply devout Puritan Christian, Milton's magnum opus 'Paradise Lost' aimed to 'justify the ways of God to men.' His works are saturated with biblical themes and theological reflection.

sources.note: Biography including his Puritan faith and theological writings

people.name: Fyodor Dostoevsky
people.title: Novelist
people.bio: Russian novelist, philosopher, and journalist. His major works include 'Crime and Punishment,' 'The Brothers Karamazov,' and 'The Idiot.'
people.faith: After a period of doubt, Dostoevsky experienced a profound renewal of faith during his imprisonment in Siberia. His novels explore the depths of human sin and the transforming power of Christ's love. He wrote, 'If anyone proved to me that Christ was outside the truth... I would remain with Christ.'

people.name: G.K. Chesterton
people.title: Author & Apologist
people.bio: English writer, philosopher, and Christian apologist. Author of the Father Brown mysteries, 'Orthodoxy,' 'The Everlasting Man,' and over 80 books.
people.faith: Initially an agnostic, Chesterton converted first to Anglicanism and then to Roman Catholicism. His book 'Orthodoxy' describes his intellectual journey to faith. C.S. Lewis credited 'The Everlasting Man' as key to his own conversion.

people.name: John Bunyan
people.title: Author & Preacher
people.bio: English writer and Puritan preacher best known for 'The Pilgrim's Progress,' one of the most significant works of English literature and the most widely read Christian allegory ever written.
people.faith: A Baptist preacher who was imprisoned for 12 years for preaching without a license. During his imprisonment he wrote 'The Pilgrim's Progress,' an allegory of the Christian journey from sin to salvation that has never been out of print.

people.name: Augustine of Hippo
people.title: Theologian & Author
people.bio: Early Christian theologian and philosopher from North Africa. Bishop of Hippo Regius and author of 'Confessions' and 'The City of God,' among the most influential works in Western Christianity.
people.faith: After years of dissolute living and exploring various philosophies, Augustine dramatically converted to Christianity, famously hearing a child's voice saying 'Take up and read.' His 'Confessions' is one of the first autobiographies in Western literature and a testament to God's grace.

people.name: Leo Tolstoy
people.title: Novelist
people.bio: Russian writer regarded as one of the greatest authors of all time. Best known for 'War and Peace' and 'Anna Karenina.'
people.faith: After a spiritual crisis in midlife, Tolstoy underwent a profound Christian conversion. He devoted the latter part of his life to understanding and following the teachings of Jesus, writing 'A Confession' and 'The Kingdom of God Is Within You.'

people.name: George MacDonald
people.title: Author & Minister
people.bio: Scottish author, poet, and Christian minister. A pioneering figure in fantasy literature, writing works such as 'Phantastes,' 'The Princess and the Goblin,' and 'Lilith.'
people.faith: A Congregationalist minister whose theology of God's love permeated all his writings. C.S. Lewis called MacDonald his 'master' and credited MacDonald's 'Phantastes' with 'baptizing his imagination.'

people.name: Isaac Newton
people.title: Physicist & Mathematician
people.bio: English mathematician, physicist, and astronomer who formulated the laws of motion and universal gravitation, developed calculus, and made groundbreaking contributions to optics.
people.faith: Newton wrote more about theology than science. He believed the elegant design of the solar system pointed to God and wrote, 'This most beautiful system of the sun, planets and comets, could only proceed from the counsel and dominion of an intelligent and powerful Being.'

people.name: Blaise Pascal
people.title: Mathematician, Physicist & Philosopher
people.bio: French mathematician, physicist, inventor, and philosopher. Made foundational contributions to probability theory, projective geometry, and the development of the mechanical calculator.
people.faith: After a mystical experience on November 23, 1654 (his 'Night of Fire'), Pascal devoted his life to defending Christianity. His 'Pensées' remains one of the most eloquent defenses of the Christian faith ever written. He is the author of 'Pascal's Wager.'

people.name: Michael Faraday
people.title: Physicist & Chemist
people.bio: English scientist who contributed to the study of electromagnetism and electrochemistry. His discoveries include electromagnetic induction, diamagnetism, and electrolysis.
people.faith: A devout member of the Sandemanian church (a small Christian sect), Faraday's faith was central to his life. He served as a deacon and elder and saw no conflict between science and his Christian beliefs, viewing the study of nature as the study of God's creation.

people.name: Gregor Mendel
people.title: Geneticist & Friar
people.bio: Augustinian friar and scientist who discovered the fundamental laws of inheritance through his work with pea plants. Known as the 'Father of Genetics.'
people.faith: An Augustinian friar who served as abbot of his monastery, Mendel's scientific work was conducted within the context of his religious life. He saw no contradiction between his faith and his scientific research.

people.name: George Washington Carver
people.title: Agricultural Scientist & Inventor
people.bio: American agricultural scientist and inventor who developed hundreds of products using peanuts, sweet potatoes, and soybeans. Born into slavery, he became one of the most prominent Black scientists of his era.
people.faith: A devout Christian who attributed all his discoveries to God. He famously said, 'I love to think of nature as an unlimited broadcasting station, through which God speaks to us every hour, if we will only tune in.' He taught a weekly Bible class for over 30 years.

people.name: Louis Pasteur
people.title: Chemist & Microbiologist
people.bio: French chemist and microbiologist renowned for his discoveries of the principles of vaccination, microbial fermentation, and pasteurization. He is known as the 'Father of Microbiology.'
people.faith: A devout Catholic who maintained his faith throughout his scientific career. He stated, 'The more I study nature, the more I stand amazed at the work of the Creator.'

people.name: Johannes Kepler
people.title: Astronomer & Mathematician
people.bio: German astronomer, mathematician, and astrologer who discovered the three laws of planetary motion, foundational to Isaac Newton's theory of universal gravitation.
people.faith: A devout Lutheran who saw his scientific work as a form of worship. He wrote, 'I was merely thinking God's thoughts after Him. Since we astronomers are priests of the highest God in regard to the book of nature, it benefits us to be thoughtful.'

people.name: Robert Boyle
people.title: Chemist & Physicist
people.bio: Anglo-Irish natural philosopher, chemist, physicist, and inventor, regarded as the first modern chemist. Best known for Boyle's Law relating gas pressure and volume.
people.faith: A deeply devout Christian who wrote extensively on theology and funded Bible translations. He established the Boyle Lectures for defending Christianity against atheism and viewed science as a way to understand God's creation.

people.name: James Clerk Maxwell
people.title: Physicist & Mathematician
people.bio: Scottish physicist responsible for the classical theory of electromagnetic radiation, unifying electricity, magnetism, and light. Einstein called Maxwell's work 'the most profound and the most fruitful that physics has experienced since the time of Newton.'
people.faith: A deeply committed evangelical Presbyterian who prayed regularly and saw his physics as revealing God's glory. He wrote, 'I think that the results which each man arrives at in his attempts to harmonize his science with his Christianity ought not to be regarded as having any significance except to the man himself.'

people.name: Francis Collins
people.title: Geneticist
people.bio: American physician-geneticist who led the Human Genome Project and served as director of the National Institutes of Health (NIH) from 2009 to 2021.
people.faith: A former atheist who converted to Christianity after reading C.S. Lewis's 'Mere Christianity.' He wrote 'The Language of God: A Scientist Presents Evidence for Belief' and founded BioLogos to explore the harmony between science and faith.

people.name: Leonhard Euler
people.title: Mathematician & Physicist
people.bio: Swiss mathematician, physicist, and astronomer who made foundational contributions to numerous areas of mathematics. He is considered one of the greatest mathematicians of all time.
people.faith: A devout Calvinist who wrote a defense of Christianity called 'Letters to a German Princess,' which included arguments for the existence of God. He held daily family prayers and composed hymns.

people.name: Bernhard Riemann
people.title: Mathematician
people.bio: German mathematician who made lasting contributions to analysis, number theory, and differential geometry, including the Riemann hypothesis and Riemannian geometry (which provided the mathematical foundation for Einstein's general relativity).
people.faith: The son of a Lutheran pastor, Riemann was a devout Christian throughout his life. He originally planned to study theology before pursuing mathematics.

sources.note: Biography including his pastoral family background and original intent to study theology

people.tagline: Laid the foundations for Einstein's relativity while trusting in God's Word

people.name: Georg Cantor
people.title: Mathematician
people.bio: German mathematician who created set theory and established the importance of one-to-one correspondence between sets, defining infinite and well-ordered sets.
people.faith: A devout Lutheran who believed his theory of transfinite numbers was communicated to him by God. He saw the absolute infinite as an expression of the nature of God.

sources.note: Biography including his belief that God communicated set theory to him

people.tagline: Explored the infinite in mathematics as a reflection of God's infinity

people.name: Augustin-Louis Cauchy
people.title: Mathematician
people.bio: French mathematician who was a pioneer of mathematical analysis. One of the most prolific mathematicians in history, he published nearly 800 research papers and made foundational contributions to calculus, group theory, and complex analysis.
people.faith: Cauchy was a deeply devout Catholic who publicly defended the faith at the French Academy of Sciences. He organized charitable work for the poor in Paris and supported Catholic missions in Ireland. He said: 'I am a Christian — that is to say, I believe in the divinity of Jesus Christ, with Tycho Brahe, Copernicus, Descartes, Newton, Leibniz, and Pascal.'

people.name: Michelangelo Buonarroti
people.title: Artist, Sculptor & Architect
people.bio: Italian sculptor, painter, architect, and poet of the High Renaissance. Created the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel, the statue of David, and the Pietà.
people.faith: A devout Catholic whose greatest works depicted biblical scenes and Christian themes. His Sistine Chapel ceiling telling the story of Genesis and his Pietà depicting Mary holding the body of Christ are among the most recognizable Christian artworks ever created.

people.name: Rembrandt van Rijn
people.title: Painter & Printmaker
people.bio: Dutch Golden Age painter and printmaker, generally considered one of the greatest visual artists in history. Known for biblical paintings, self-portraits, and masterful use of light and shadow.
people.faith: A deeply devout Protestant Christian, Rembrandt painted over 300 biblical scenes, more than any other major Western artist. His 'The Return of the Prodigal Son' is considered one of the most powerful visual depictions of God's grace.

people.name: Albrecht Dürer
people.title: Artist & Printmaker
people.bio: German painter, printmaker, and theorist of the German Renaissance. Known for his woodcuts, engravings, and paintings including 'Praying Hands.'
people.faith: A devout Christian who embraced the Protestant Reformation. His famous 'Praying Hands' drawing and numerous biblical engravings reflect his deep faith. He admired Martin Luther and incorporated Reformation theology into his later works.

people.name: Antoni Gaudí
people.title: Architect
people.bio: Spanish architect and the best-known practitioner of Catalan Modernism. His masterwork, the Sagrada Família basilica in Barcelona, remains under construction more than a century after his death.
people.faith: A devout Catholic whose later years were entirely dedicated to building the Sagrada Família as an offering to God. He lived ascetically and attended Mass daily. He is currently a candidate for canonization in the Catholic Church.

people.name: Martin Luther
people.title: Reformer & Theologian
people.bio: German professor of theology, priest, and seminal figure in the Protestant Reformation. His Ninety-five Theses challenged the Catholic Church's practices and launched the Reformation.
people.faith: Luther's rediscovery of justification by faith alone (sola fide) through Scripture alone (sola scriptura) transformed Christianity. His translation of the Bible into German made Scripture accessible to ordinary people.

people.name: John Calvin
people.title: Reformer & Theologian
people.bio: French theologian, pastor, and reformer who was a principal figure in the development of the system of Christian theology later called Calvinism. His 'Institutes of the Christian Religion' is a foundational work of Protestant theology.
people.faith: Calvin's theology emphasized the sovereignty of God, the authority of Scripture, and salvation by grace alone through faith alone. His work in Geneva established a model for Reformed churches worldwide.

people.title: Theologian & Preacher
people.bio: American revivalist preacher, philosopher, and Congregationalist Protestant theologian. Key figure in the First Great Awakening and third president of Princeton University.
people.faith: Edwards is widely regarded as America's greatest theologian. His sermon 'Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God' is one of the most famous sermons in American history. His theological works on the nature of true virtue and religious affections remain deeply influential.

people.title: Preacher & Author
people.bio: English Particular Baptist preacher known as the 'Prince of Preachers.' Pastor of the Metropolitan Tabernacle in London, he frequently preached to audiences of more than 10,000 people.
people.faith: Spurgeon was converted at age 15 and began preaching immediately. He published thousands of sermons and authored numerous books. He founded a pastors' college, orphanages, and other charitable organizations, all flowing from his deep love for Christ.

people.name: John Wesley
people.title: Theologian & Evangelist
people.bio: English cleric, theologian, and evangelist who co-founded the Methodist movement. His open-air preaching brought the gospel to millions across England.
people.faith: After his famous 'Aldersgate experience' in 1738 where his heart was 'strangely warmed,' Wesley dedicated his life to evangelism. He traveled 250,000 miles on horseback, preached over 40,000 sermons, and sparked a revival that transformed England.

people.title: Evangelist & Preacher
people.bio: Anglican cleric and evangelist who was one of the founders of Methodism and the evangelical movement. He preached to enormous crowds on both sides of the Atlantic and was a key figure in the Great Awakening.
people.faith: Whitefield was one of the most powerful preachers in Christian history, known for his dramatic delivery and deep conviction. Benjamin Franklin estimated he could be heard by 30,000 people at once. He preached over 18,000 sermons in his lifetime.

people.title: Politician & Reformer
people.bio: British politician and philanthropist who led the movement to abolish the slave trade in the British Empire. Member of Parliament for 45 years.
people.faith: After his evangelical conversion in 1785, Wilberforce devoted his life to two 'great objects': the abolition of the slave trade and the reformation of manners (moral reform). His Christian faith was the driving force behind his tireless campaign against slavery.

people.name: Dietrich Bonhoeffer
people.title: Theologian & Martyr
people.bio: German Lutheran pastor, theologian, and anti-Nazi dissident. Author of 'The Cost of Discipleship' and 'Life Together.' Executed by the Nazis at Flossenbürg concentration camp.
people.faith: Bonhoeffer's theology of 'costly grace' challenged cheap Christianity. He helped found the Confessing Church in opposition to Nazi influence and participated in a plot to assassinate Hitler. His final words were, 'This is the end — for me, the beginning of life.'

people.name: John Wycliffe
people.title: Theologian & Proto-Reformer
people.bio: English theologian and professor at Oxford, known as the 'Morning Star of the Reformation.' He produced one of the first complete English translations of the Bible and challenged papal authority.
people.faith: Wycliffe taught that Scripture alone is the supreme authority for Christian life and doctrine — anticipating sola scriptura by 150 years. He trained lay preachers (Lollards) to spread the gospel in common English. The church declared him a heretic posthumously and exhumed his bones to burn them.

people.name: Thomas Aquinas
people.title: Theologian & Philosopher
people.bio: Italian Dominican friar and the most influential medieval theologian. His Summa Theologica remains one of the greatest works of systematic theology ever written, synthesizing Aristotelian philosophy with Christian doctrine.
people.faith: Aquinas devoted his entire life to understanding and articulating the Christian faith through reason and revelation. Near the end of his life, after a mystical experience during Mass, he stopped writing, saying: 'All that I have written seems like straw compared to what has been revealed to me.'

people.name: Athanasius of Alexandria
people.title: Church Father & Defender of Trinitarian Doctrine
people.bio: Bishop of Alexandria who spent most of his life fighting Arianism — the heresy that Christ was a created being. Exiled five times by four different emperors for his unwavering defense of Christ's full divinity.
people.faith: Athanasius stood virtually alone against the world (Athanasius contra mundum) in defending the Nicene Creed and the full deity of Christ. His work On the Incarnation remains a classic of Christian theology. C.S. Lewis wrote the introduction to a modern translation.

people.name: John Knox
people.title: Scottish Reformer
people.bio: Scottish clergyman who led the Protestant Reformation in Scotland and founded the Presbyterian Church. His fiery preaching transformed an entire nation's religious landscape.
people.faith: Knox prayed, 'Give me Scotland, or I die!' His zeal for Reformed worship and church governance shaped Scottish Christianity for centuries. Mary Queen of Scots reportedly said she feared Knox's prayers more than all the armies of England.

people.name: Abraham Kuyper
people.title: Theologian, Statesman & Prime Minister
people.bio: Dutch Reformed theologian who served as Prime Minister of the Netherlands (1901–1905). Founded the Free University of Amsterdam and a new Reformed denomination. A towering intellectual who applied Calvinist worldview to every sphere of life.
people.faith: Kuyper declared: 'There is not a square inch in the whole domain of our human existence over which Christ, who is Sovereign over all, does not cry, Mine!' His vision of Christ's lordship over all of life continues to shape Reformed thought worldwide.

people.name: Martyn Lloyd-Jones
people.title: Preacher & Pastor
people.bio: Welsh Protestant minister who left a promising career in medicine to preach. Served as pastor of Westminster Chapel in London for 30 years, becoming one of the most influential preachers of the 20th century.
people.faith: Lloyd-Jones was a passionate defender of expository preaching and Reformed theology. His multi-volume studies on Romans and Ephesians, and his book Preaching and Preachers, have shaped generations of pastors. He emphasized the necessity of experiencing God's power, not merely knowing about Him.

people.title: Theologian & Author
people.bio: British-born Canadian Reformed theologian and author of Knowing God, one of the best-selling Christian books of the 20th century. He helped shape modern evangelicalism while maintaining deep Reformed convictions.
people.faith: Packer's Knowing God made profound theology accessible to ordinary believers. He emphasized that knowing God personally — not merely knowing about Him — is the whole purpose of human existence. A humble scholar who once said theology was simply 'thinking about God in His presence.'

people.name: Herman Bavinck
people.title: Dutch Reformed Theologian
people.bio: Dutch Reformed theologian whose four-volume Reformed Dogmatics is considered one of the greatest systematic theologies ever written. Professor at Kampen and the Free University of Amsterdam.
people.faith: Bavinck's vision was to engage modern thought without compromising Reformed orthodoxy. His Reformed Dogmatics masterfully integrates biblical theology with philosophy and culture. He wrote: 'The essence of the Christian religion consists in the reality that the creation of the Father, devastated by sin, is restored in the death of the Son.'

people.name: B.B. Warfield
people.title: Princeton Theologian
people.bio: Professor of theology at Princeton Seminary for over 30 years. The last great Princeton theologian before the liberal reorganization, he was the foremost defender of biblical inerrancy in his era.
people.faith: Warfield's defense of the inspiration and inerrancy of Scripture laid the theological foundation that continues to undergird evangelical and Reformed churches. He argued that Christianity is not merely a feeling but is grounded in historical facts and propositional truth. He also tenderly cared for his invalid wife for decades.

people.name: Al Mohler
people.title: Theologian & Seminary President
people.bio: President of The Southern Baptist Theological Seminary since 1993, the flagship seminary of the Southern Baptist Convention. A leading public intellectual and daily podcaster who addresses theology, culture, and current events.
people.faith: Mohler led the conservative resurgence at Southern Seminary, returning it to its confessional roots. His daily podcast The Briefing and his prolific writing have made him one of the most influential Reformed voices in American Christianity. He champions biblical inerrancy, complementarianism, and the sufficiency of Scripture.

people.title: Evangelist
people.bio: American evangelist and ordained Southern Baptist minister whose broadcasts and world tours spanning over six decades made him one of the most influential Christian leaders of the 20th century. He preached to live audiences of nearly 215 million people in more than 185 countries.
people.faith: Known as 'America's Pastor,' Graham preached a simple message of salvation through Jesus Christ. He served as spiritual advisor to multiple U.S. Presidents and founded the Billy Graham Evangelistic Association. His integrity and humility set a standard for public Christian ministry.

people.title: Missionary & Martyr
people.bio: American Christian missionary who was killed along with four companions during Operation Auca, an attempt to evangelize the Huaorani people of Ecuador. He was 28 years old. His death galvanized a generation of missionaries.
people.faith: Elliot famously wrote in his journal, 'He is no fool who gives what he cannot keep to gain what he cannot lose.' His wife Elisabeth Elliot later returned to minister to the very tribe that killed him, and many Huaorani eventually became Christians.

people.name: Nate Saint
people.title: Missionary Pilot & Martyr
people.tagline: Missionary pilot martyred alongside Jim Elliot — his son Steve later befriended the very tribe that killed his father
people.bio: Nate Saint was an American missionary pilot who served with Mission Aviation Fellowship (MAF) in Ecuador. A gifted inventor, he developed the 'bucket drop' technique to safely deliver gifts to unreached tribes from the air. He was part of Operation Auca, the effort to make contact with the isolated Waorani (Auca) people. On January 8, 1956, he and four companions — Jim Elliot, Ed McCully, Pete Fleming, and Roger Youderian — were killed by Waorani warriors on a sandbar along the Curaray River. His son Steve later lived among the Waorani and was baptized by the very men who had killed his father.
people.faith: Saint's faith drove him to risk his life bringing the gospel to unreached peoples. His inventive use of aviation technology in missionary service demonstrated a practical, resourceful faith. The remarkable sequel to his martyrdom — his son Steve's reconciliation with the Waorani and the eventual conversion of many in the tribe — stands as one of the most powerful testimonies of forgiveness and redemption in modern missions history.

sources.note: By Russell Hitt. Biography of Nate Saint's missionary aviation work

people.name: Ed McCully
people.title: Missionary & Martyr
people.tagline: Gave up a promising legal career to bring the gospel to Ecuador — martyred at age 28
people.bio: Ed McCully was an American missionary who gave up a promising career as an attorney and orator to serve in Ecuador with the Plymouth Brethren. A former college football star and championship orator at Wheaton College, McCully felt called to missions after hearing Jim Elliot speak. He and his wife Marilou served the Quichua people before joining Operation Auca. He was among the five missionaries killed by Waorani warriors on January 8, 1956.
people.faith: McCully's conversion from worldly ambition to missionary service exemplifies the radical call of the gospel. He wrote to Jim Elliot: 'I have one desire now — to live a life of reckless abandon for the Lord, putting all my energy and strength into it.' His willingness to leave behind comfort, career, and ultimately his life for the sake of unreached peoples reflects the costly discipleship Jesus described.

sources.title: Through Gates of Splendor
sources.note: By Elisabeth Elliot. The definitive account of Operation Auca

people.name: Pete Fleming
people.title: Missionary Linguist & Martyr
people.tagline: Linguist and scholar who chose the mission field over academia — martyred bringing the gospel to the Waorani
people.bio: Pete Fleming was an American missionary and linguist who served in Ecuador. A graduate of the University of Washington with a master's degree in literature, Fleming had a gift for languages and was instrumental in early attempts to learn the Waorani language. He married Olive Ainslie in Ecuador, and together they served among the Quichua people. His linguistic skills were vital to Operation Auca's efforts to communicate with the isolated Waorani. He was killed alongside his four companions on January 8, 1956.
people.faith: Fleming wrestled deeply with God's call on his life, recording his spiritual journey in detailed journals. He wrote: 'I am not afraid of death. I am afraid of not living.' His decision to pursue missions over an academic career reflected a conviction that the gospel of Jesus Christ was the most important message the world could hear. His careful, scholarly approach to missionary work modeled the integration of intellectual gifts and gospel passion.

sources.title: Through Gates of Splendor
sources.note: By Elisabeth Elliot. Includes Pete Fleming's story

people.name: Roger Youderian
people.title: Missionary & Martyr
people.tagline: WWII paratrooper and Purple Heart recipient who gave his life as a missionary to the Waorani
people.bio: Roger Youderian was an American missionary and decorated World War II veteran who served with the Jívaro (Shuar) people in Ecuador. A paratrooper who fought in the Battle of the Bulge and received the Purple Heart, Youderian brought the same courage and discipline to the mission field. He struggled with discouragement in his work among the Jívaro before being invited to join Operation Auca. He initially hesitated, feeling unworthy, but ultimately joined the team. He was killed with his four companions on January 8, 1956.
people.faith: Youderian's faith was forged in the fires of combat and refined through seasons of missionary discouragement. His journal entries reveal a man who honestly wrestled with feelings of failure and inadequacy, yet continually returned to trust in God's sovereignty. His decision to join Operation Auca came after a spiritual breakthrough. He wrote in his journal the night before the fatal contact: 'I am called and I must go.' His story reminds us that God uses broken, doubting people for His greatest purposes.

people.faith: Taylor pioneered a new approach to missions by adopting Chinese dress and customs, living by faith without soliciting funds, and opening inland China to the gospel. The CIM he founded became the largest mission agency in the world, eventually bringing over 800 missionaries to China.

people.faith: Liddell famously said, 'God made me fast. And when I run, I feel His pleasure.' After his Olympic triumph, he devoted his life to missionary work in China. His story was immortalized in the Academy Award-winning film 'Chariots of Fire.'

people.faith: Carey's famous motto was 'Expect great things from God; attempt great things for God.' He labored for decades in India, translating the Bible into Bengali, Sanskrit, and many other languages, establishing schools, and fighting social evils like sati (widow burning).

people.name: Francis Schaeffer
people.title: Philosopher, Theologian & Author
people.bio: American evangelical theologian, philosopher, and Presbyterian pastor. He founded the L'Abri Fellowship community in Switzerland, which became a haven for seekers and intellectuals exploring Christian faith. He authored influential works including 'The God Who Is There' and 'How Should We Then Live?'
people.faith: Schaeffer engaged the intellectual culture of the 20th century with a rigorous Christian worldview. He argued that Christianity provides the only adequate basis for human meaning, morality, and knowledge. His work influenced a generation of evangelical thinkers and leaders.

people.name: John Piper
people.title: Pastor & Theologian
people.bio: American Reformed Baptist pastor, author, and theologian. He served as pastor of Bethlehem Baptist Church in Minneapolis for 33 years and founded Desiring God Ministries. He has authored over 50 books including 'Desiring God' and 'Don't Waste Your Life.'
people.faith: Piper is known for his concept of 'Christian Hedonism' — the idea that 'God is most glorified in us when we are most satisfied in Him.' His expository preaching, passionate delivery, and emphasis on God's sovereignty have shaped a generation of Reformed evangelicals.

people.name: A.W. Tozer
people.title: Pastor & Author
people.bio: American Christian pastor, author, magazine editor, and spiritual mentor. He served as pastor for 44 years, primarily at Southside Alliance Church in Chicago. Despite having no formal education, he received honorary doctorates from Wheaton and Houghton colleges.
people.faith: Tozer's devotional writings emphasize the pursuit of God's presence above all else. His books 'The Pursuit of God' and 'The Knowledge of the Holy' are considered spiritual classics. He was known as a 'prophet' who called the church to deeper intimacy with God.

people.name: Lottie Moon
people.title: Missionary to China
people.bio: American Southern Baptist missionary who served in China for nearly 40 years. She spent her life evangelizing and advocating for mission support. The annual Lottie Moon Christmas Offering for international missions, named in her honor, has raised billions of dollars.
people.faith: Moon gave everything for the gospel in China, eventually dying of starvation because she had given all her food to starving Chinese during a famine. Her sacrificial example inspired the Southern Baptist Convention's largest missions offering, still collected annually.

people.name: Watchman Nee
people.title: Church Leader & Author
people.bio: Chinese church leader and Christian author who founded the Local Church movement in China. He authored influential works including 'The Normal Christian Life' and 'Sit, Walk, Stand.' He was imprisoned by the Communist government in 1952 and died in prison after 20 years.
people.faith: Nee's emphasis on the inner spiritual life and the practical experience of Christ's indwelling has influenced millions of Christians worldwide. He endured two decades of imprisonment for his faith without renouncing Christ.

people.name: Charles Finney
people.title: Evangelist & Revivalist
people.bio: American Presbyterian minister, revivalist leader, and president of Oberlin College. He was a central figure in the Second Great Awakening and is estimated to have led over 500,000 people to Christ.
people.faith: Finney's dramatic conversion experience in 1821 transformed him from a lawyer to a fiery evangelist. His revival meetings featured new methods including the 'anxious bench' and extended meetings. He was also a strong advocate for abolition and women's education.

people.name: William Booth
people.title: Evangelist & Social Reformer
people.bio: English Methodist preacher who co-founded The Salvation Army with his wife Catherine in 1865. The organization combined evangelism with social services for the poor and has grown into a worldwide movement operating in over 130 countries.
people.faith: Booth's passion was bringing the gospel to the poorest and most neglected members of society. He famously said, 'While women weep, as they do now, I'll fight; while little children go hungry, as they do now, I'll fight.' The Salvation Army's model of practical Christianity has served millions.

people.name: Gladys Aylward
people.title: Missionary to China
people.bio: English missionary to China who served from 1930 onward. During the Japanese invasion, she led over 100 orphan children on a harrowing journey across the mountains to safety. Her story was told in the film 'The Inn of the Sixth Happiness.'
people.faith: Rejected by the China Inland Mission for lacking education, Aylward saved her own money to travel to China by the Trans-Siberian Railway. She adopted Chinese citizenship, ran an inn for muleteers where she told Bible stories, and became known as 'Ai-weh-deh' (the Virtuous One).

people.name: Tim Keller
people.title: Pastor, Author & Apologist
people.bio: Founding pastor of Redeemer Presbyterian Church in Manhattan, which grew from a small group to over 5,000 members. Author of The Reason for God, The Prodigal God, and numerous other influential books bridging Reformed theology and urban culture.
people.faith: Keller made Reformed theology accessible to skeptics and seekers in one of the most secular cities in America. He demonstrated that rigorous theological thinking and compassionate cultural engagement are not opposites. Near the end of his life, battling cancer, he wrote about his confidence in the resurrection: 'There is no party like the one we are going to.'

people.name: John Owen
people.title: Puritan Theologian
people.bio: English Nonconformist theologian, Vice-Chancellor of Oxford University, and chaplain to Oliver Cromwell. Often called 'the prince of Puritan theologians' for the depth and precision of his doctrinal works.
people.faith: Owen's works on the Holy Spirit, communion with God, and the mortification of sin remain unsurpassed in depth. He wrote: 'Be killing sin or it will be killing you.' His treatise The Death of Death in the Death of Christ is a definitive defense of particular redemption.

people.name: Richard Baxter
people.title: Puritan Pastor & Author
people.bio: English Puritan leader who pastored in Kidderminster and transformed the town through faithful ministry. His prolific writing produced over 140 books, including The Reformed Pastor and The Saints' Everlasting Rest.
people.faith: Baxter's ministry in Kidderminster turned a spiritually apathetic town into a community where 'you could hear families singing psalms and praying from every house.' The Reformed Pastor remains essential reading for ministers. He modeled what it means to shepherd souls with tireless devotion.

people.name: John Flavel
people.title: Puritan Pastor & Author
people.bio: English Puritan clergyman known for his devotional writing and pastoral warmth. He ministered in Dartmouth, England, and his works were widely read for their deep piety and practical application.
people.faith: Flavel's Keeping the Heart is considered one of the finest Puritan devotional works. He wrote: 'The soul of religion is the practical part.' Ejected from his pulpit by the Act of Uniformity, he continued preaching in secret — sometimes in the woods, sometimes at sea — risking imprisonment for the gospel.

people.name: Thomas Watson
people.title: Puritan Preacher & Author
people.bio: English Nonconformist Puritan preacher and author, known for his clear and practical expositions of doctrine. Ejected from his pulpit by the Great Ejection of 1662.
people.faith: Watson's A Body of Divinity and The Doctrine of Repentance are prized for making deep theology vivid and memorable. He wrote: 'God's wounds cure, sin's kisses kill.' His gift was making the truths of the Westminster Shorter Catechism come alive for ordinary believers.

people.name: Matthew Henry
people.title: Bible Commentator & Pastor
people.bio: Welsh-born Nonconformist minister whose Commentary on the Whole Bible remains one of the most widely used Bible commentaries ever written — over 300 years after its publication.
people.faith: Henry's commentary is treasured for its devotional warmth and practical wisdom. Spurgeon said every minister should read it through at least once. Henry wrote: 'The joy of the Lord will arm us against the assaults of our spiritual enemies and put our mouths out of taste for those pleasures with which the tempter baits his hooks.'

people.name: Charlie Soong
people.title: Industrialist, Publisher & Methodist Leader
people.bio: Chinese-American industrialist and Methodist missionary who became one of the wealthiest men in China. Born Han Jiaozhun, he studied at Vanderbilt University and Duke, then returned to China where he printed and distributed Bibles.
people.faith: Soong converted to Christianity as a young man in America and was baptized a Methodist. He devoted himself to printing Bibles and Christian literature for China, becoming the foremost Bible publisher in the country. His faith shaped his entire family — his children became some of the most influential figures in modern China, and his daughter Mei-ling led Chiang Kai-shek to Christ.

people.name: Sun Yat-sen
people.title: Revolutionary & Father of Modern China
people.bio: Chinese revolutionary and political leader who served as the first president of the Republic of China. Known as the 'Father of the Nation,' he overthrew the Qing dynasty and established a republic.
people.faith: Sun was baptized as a Christian in Hong Kong in 1883 and credited Christianity as a driving force behind his revolutionary ideals of equality and justice. He said: 'I am a follower of Jesus Christ. The Revolution has its origins in Christianity.' His Three Principles of the People were influenced by Christian ethics.

people.name: Chiang Kai-shek
people.title: Military & Political Leader
people.bio: Leader of the Republic of China who governed mainland China before retreating to Taiwan. He led China through World War II and was one of the most consequential figures in 20th-century Asian history.
people.faith: Chiang was baptized a Methodist Christian in 1930, influenced by his wife Soong Mei-ling. He attributed his survival through war and exile to God's providence. He read the Bible daily and wrote in his diary: 'My faith is my greatest source of strength.'

people.name: Soong Mei-ling
people.title: First Lady of the Republic of China
people.bio: Known as Madame Chiang Kai-shek, she was a political figure, diplomat, and painter who addressed the U.S. Congress and played a key role in Sino-American relations. She lived to 105.
people.faith: Raised in a devout Methodist family (one of the famous Soong sisters), Mei-ling's faith shaped her public service and diplomacy. She influenced her husband's conversion and was known for her public witness. She famously said: 'I can say that God has led me — and He does lead me.'

people.name: Pandita Ramabai
people.title: Scholar, Reformer & Evangelist
people.bio: Indian social reformer, scholar, and champion of women's rights who was the first woman to be awarded the title Pandita (scholar) for her mastery of Sanskrit. She founded the Mukti Mission to rescue child brides, widows, and orphans.
people.faith: Born a high-caste Hindu, Ramabai converted to Christianity in England in 1883 after being deeply moved by the love she witnessed among Christians. She translated the Bible into Marathi and led a remarkable revival at Mukti Mission in 1905. She said: 'Christ has set me free.'

people.name: Sadhu Sundar Singh
people.title: Indian Evangelist & Mystic
people.bio: Known as the 'Apostle with Bleeding Feet,' Sundar Singh was a Sikh who converted to Christianity at age 15 after a vision of Christ. He wandered India barefoot in the saffron robes of a sadhu (holy man), preaching the gospel.
people.faith: After his conversion, Sundar Singh was disowned by his family and poisoned by his uncle. He adopted the life of a wandering sadhu to present Christ in a way culturally accessible to Indians. He disappeared in 1929 while on a mission trip to Tibet. He said: 'Without Christ I was like a fish out of water; with Christ I am in the ocean of love.'

people.name: Samuel Morse
people.title: Inventor of the Telegraph
people.bio: American inventor and painter who developed the electric telegraph and Morse code, revolutionizing long-distance communication. Originally trained as an artist, he turned to invention after personal tragedy.
people.faith: Morse was a devout Calvinist. The first message sent by telegraph in 1844 was 'What hath God wrought' (Numbers 23:23) — chosen by Annie Ellsworth. Morse himself said: 'The nearer I approach the end of my pilgrimage... the grandeur and sublimity of God's remedy for fallen man are more appreciated.'

people.name: Cyrus McCormick
people.title: Inventor & Philanthropist
people.bio: American inventor of the mechanical reaper, which revolutionized agriculture and helped feed growing populations. Founded the McCormick Harvesting Machine Company (later International Harvester).
people.faith: McCormick was a devout Presbyterian who gave generously to churches and theological seminaries. He was a major benefactor of what became McCormick Theological Seminary in Chicago. He saw his inventions as a way to serve God by serving humanity's need for food.

people.name: Keith & Kristyn Getty
people.years: Keith: December 16, 1974 – present; Kristyn: January 11, 1980 – present
people.title: Hymn Writers
people.tagline: Leading the modern hymn renaissance with timeless songs of faith
people.bio: Northern Irish husband-and-wife hymn writers who have become the foremost voices in modern hymn writing. Best known for co-writing 'In Christ Alone' (with Stuart Townend), as well as 'The Power of the Cross' and 'Speak O Lord.' They host the annual Sing! conference and have led a global movement to restore congregational hymn singing.
people.faith: The Gettys write hymns rooted in rich theological truth, aiming to teach doctrine through song in the tradition of Isaac Watts and Charles Wesley. Their mission is to equip the global church with songs that are biblically faithful, musically accessible, and enduring. Keith has said their goal is to help people 'sing the story of the gospel with depth and passion.'

people.name: Stuart Townend
people.title: Hymn Writer & Worship Leader
people.tagline: Penned some of the most beloved modern hymns in the English-speaking church
people.bio: English Christian hymn writer and worship leader whose compositions are sung in churches worldwide. He co-wrote 'In Christ Alone' with Keith Getty, and is also known for 'How Deep the Father's Love for Us' and 'The Power of the Cross.' His hymns blend poetic craftsmanship with deep theological content.
people.faith: Townend's hymns are marked by their doctrinal richness and scriptural fidelity, drawing deeply from the Reformed tradition. He has spoken of his desire to write songs that help congregations grasp the truths of Scripture in a way that moves both mind and heart. His work has helped bridge contemporary worship and traditional hymnody across denominations.

people.faith: Shane & Shane are committed to creating worship music rooted directly in Scripture, often setting entire Psalm passages to melody. Their ministry reflects a deep conviction that God's Word should be central in corporate worship and personal devotion. They have discipled a generation of young believers through their ministry at The Porch and their extensive catalog of scripture songs.

people.tagline: Teaching the church to worship with both spirit and truth
people.bio: Director of worship development for Sovereign Grace Churches and a former member of the Christian band Glad. He is the author of 'Worship Matters' and 'True Worshipers,' influential books on biblical worship philosophy and practice. Kauflin has shaped worship theology and practice across the Reformed evangelical world.
people.faith: Kauflin's ministry is driven by the conviction that worship must be grounded in sound theology and empowered by the Holy Spirit. He has spent decades training worship leaders to prioritize God's glory over musical performance. His writing and teaching emphasize that true worship flows from a heart captivated by the gospel of Jesus Christ.

people.bio: Pastor and prolific songwriter associated with Sovereign Grace Churches. He is best known for writing 'I Stand in Awe,' a worship song that has been sung across denominations worldwide. Altrogge has authored hundreds of worship songs combining Reformed theology with heartfelt devotion.
people.faith: Altrogge's songs reflect deep Reformed conviction and a pastor's heart for helping congregations see the greatness of God. His hymn 'I Stand in Awe' captures the wonder of God's holiness and grace in a way that has resonated with believers for decades. He continues to serve as a pastor while writing songs that exalt Christ and edify the church.

people.title: Worship Leader & Songwriter
people.tagline: Wrote 'Jesus, Thank You' — a beloved communion hymn in Reformed churches
people.bio: Sovereign Grace worship leader and songwriter known for penning 'Jesus, Thank You,' one of the most widely sung communion hymns in Reformed and evangelical churches. His songs are marked by a clear proclamation of the gospel and the finished work of Christ on the cross.
people.faith: Sczebel's songwriting reflects a deep gratitude for the substitutionary atonement of Christ, beautifully expressed in 'Jesus, Thank You' with its vivid portrayal of what Christ accomplished at Calvary. His work within Sovereign Grace Churches has contributed to a rich body of theologically grounded worship music. His songs help congregations respond to the gospel with wonder and thanksgiving.

people.name: Matt Papa
people.title: Worship Leader, Songwriter & Author
people.tagline: Co-writer of 'His Mercy Is More' — modern hymnwriter fueling the Christ-centered worship movement
people.bio: Matt Papa is a worship leader, songwriter, and author based in Nashville who has become one of the leading voices in the modern hymn movement. He co-wrote 'His Mercy Is More' with Matt Boswell, which has become one of the most beloved hymns in contemporary Reformed worship. Papa frequently leads worship at Together for the Gospel (T4G) and Getty Music conferences, and has released multiple albums of theologically rich worship music. He is also the author of 'Look and Live: Behold the Soul-Thrilling, Sin-Destroying Glory of Christ.'
people.faith: Papa's music and writing are saturated with a vision of God's glory as the ultimate satisfaction of the human soul. His song 'His Mercy Is More' captures the boundless, persevering mercy of God toward sinners — a theme drawn from Lamentations 3. He is passionate about worship that exalts Christ above all and has spoken extensively about the danger of idolatry and the supremacy of God's beauty over all earthly treasures.

people.name: Matt Boswell
people.title: Pastor, Hymnwriter & Worship Leader
people.tagline: Co-writer of 'His Mercy Is More' and 'Come Behold the Wondrous Mystery' — pastor-hymnwriter shaping modern congregational worship
people.bio: Matt Boswell is a pastor, hymnwriter, and worship leader who serves as Pastor of Worship and Arts at The Village Church in the Dallas-Fort Worth area. He is one of the most prolific modern hymnwriters in the Reformed tradition, co-writing 'His Mercy Is More' and 'Come Behold the Wondrous Mystery' with Matt Papa. Boswell is the founder of the Doxology & Theology conference and the editor of 'Treasuring Christ When Your Hands Are Full.' He frequently collaborates with Keith & Kristyn Getty and leads worship at major Reformed conferences.
people.faith: Boswell is deeply committed to the local church as the primary context for worship and has written extensively on the theology of congregational singing. His hymns are marked by rich Trinitarian theology, vivid portrayals of Christ's work, and accessible melodies that serve the gathered church. 'Come Behold the Wondrous Mystery' walks through the entire arc of redemption from incarnation to resurrection, while 'His Mercy Is More' proclaims the inexhaustible grace of God.

people.faith: A practicing Christian who has been a member of various church communities. He has attributed his perseverance through FedEx's early financial crises to his faith and determination.

people.name: Alan Barnhart
people.title: CEO, Barnhart Crane & Rigging
people.bio: CEO of Barnhart Crane & Rigging, one of the largest heavy lift and transport companies in North America with over 70 locations. Alan and his brother Eric grew up working in the steel-erecting business their father founded in Memphis, Tennessee in 1969. Alan studied civil engineering and joined the business full-time in the early 1980s.
people.faith: Alan and his brother Eric made the extraordinary decision to give 100% of their highly successful company to the National Christian Foundation in 2007 to keep wealth from taking over their lives. Though they still run daily operations, the brothers will never reap the company's accrued value — they kept none of it. About 50% of all company earnings are donated immediately to charity, with the remaining 50% reinvested to grow the business. Alan has said the decision was driven by a conviction that everything belongs to God. He and his wife Katherine have six children and reside in Tennessee.

people.name: Donald Knuth
people.title: Computer Scientist & Professor Emeritus at Stanford
people.bio: American computer scientist, Professor Emeritus at Stanford University, and Turing Award winner (1974). Author of the monumental multi-volume work The Art of Computer Programming, creator of TeX and METAFONT. Widely regarded as the father of the analysis of algorithms.
people.faith: A devout Lutheran who has been outspoken about his faith throughout his career. Knuth authored 3:16 Bible Texts Illuminated, a book analyzing the '3:16' verse from every book of the Bible with calligraphic art. He delivered a series of lectures at MIT titled 'Things a Computer Scientist Rarely Talks About,' exploring the relationship between faith and computer science. He has said, 'God is real, unless declared integer.'

people.name: John Vlissides
people.title: Software Engineer & Design Patterns Co-Author
people.bio: American software engineer and one of the 'Gang of Four' who co-authored Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software, one of the most influential books in computer science. He worked at IBM's T.J. Watson Research Center and held a PhD from Stanford. He passed away on Thanksgiving 2005 at age 44 from a brain tumor.
people.faith: A bold, outspoken Christian who dedicated his portion of the Design Patterns book with 'Joshua 24:15b — J.V.' ('As for me and my house, we will serve the LORD'). When asked about his faith by colleague Ken Auer, Vlissides replied: 'Yes, I am a Christian. The fundamentalist, politically incorrect kind that believes the Bible is the inerrant word of God and that we are all sinners that are going to burn in hell unless we put our faith completely in Jesus Christ who is the only way to heaven. You know, the kind that the media thinks is the most dangerous kind.' He served as an elder at his church and later as chairman of Christian Education. During his battle with cancer, he told visitors 'God is good all the time' and drew strength from Isaiah 43:1–5. His wife Dru Ann wrote: 'John trusted those words, He lived those words. He knew God's character and he had peace in the midst of his circumstances. He IS in a better place.'

people.name: Jimmy Carter
people.title: 39th President of the United States
people.bio: American politician and humanitarian who served as the 39th President of the United States from 1977 to 1981. After leaving office, he became one of the most active former presidents in history, founding the Carter Center and dedicating decades to Habitat for Humanity, conflict resolution, and global health.
people.faith: A lifelong Southern Baptist and born-again Christian whose faith was the defining force of his life. He taught Sunday school at Maranatha Baptist Church in Plains, Georgia for over 40 years — continuing well into his 90s. He once said, 'My faith demands that I do whatever I can, wherever I am, whenever I can, for as long as I can with whatever I have to try to make a difference.' His post-presidency of humble service became one of the most powerful Christian testimonies in American political history.
